data.title: FDA ready to allow compounding pharmacies to make injectable peptides - report
data.description:
The U.S. FDA appears poised to allow compounding pharmacies to again sell injectable peptides, a class of products with questionable anti-aging and anti-disease benefits that came under scrutiny for potential safety risks.

If the agency proceeds, it will be a victory for HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who has publicly voiced his support for the products.

The FDA's list of banned peptides was last updated in 2023. One of those peptides, cesium chloride, can cause irregular heartbeat. Another, chloral hydrate, is associated with dosing errors and oversedation. Many others pose a risk for immunogenicity, a reference to triggering the body's immune response.

A senior HHS official told The New York Times that many high-ranking FDA staff do not support the change. The same official said that the FDA will go after firms that make false or misleading claims about the health benefits of their products.

The Times also said that a meeting of the agency's Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee will likely be scheduled to discuss whether there is medical evidence for the peptides and vote on whether compounding pharmacies can resume producing them.

On a February podcast with Joe Rogan, RFK Jr. said he has personally used injectable peptides for injuries with positive results.

Scott Brunner, CEO of the Alliance for Pharmacy Compounding, told the Times of his concerns with the peptides individuals are currently buying online. "The patients who are getting them are almost certainly getting either unproven, illicit product from overseas or research-grade product because there's not a version of these drugs that is pharmaceutical grade from FDA-registered manufacturers and eligible for use in humans."

Scripps Research Translational Institute Director Eric Topol added, "It's a mess, because we don’t have any data that these work."

data.title: Mkango secures £10M fundraise; trading to resume post halt
data.description:
• Mkango Resources (MKA:CA) reportedly announced a proposed equity fundraise of ~£10M (≈ C$18.4 million) through multiple offerings, placements, and subscriptions.

  • New common shares will be priced at 33 pence per share (≈ C$0.606) under the offering.

  • The £10M proceeds will be used as follows: £4.3M for German magnet acquisition, £2.7M for German capex, £0.95M for UK operations, and £2M for working capital.

  • It also shared 2027 projections, including expected selling prices of $55–70/kg and production costs between $22–36/kg across different regions (UK, Germany, US).

  • Trading of Mkango Resources (MKA:CA) shares was halted by CIRO pending the release of this announcement and is expected to resume on April 1, 2026.

  • As of March 31, 2026, shares fell 5.06% to GBX 37.50.

data.title: Foreign central bank holdings of Treasuries at the NY Fed at the lowest level since 2012
data.description:
Foreign central banks have slashed their holdings of Treasuries at the New York Federal Reserve by $82B since Feb. 25, bringing total holdings to $2.7T—the lowest level since 2012, according to Fed data, FT reported.

“The foreign official sector is selling Treasuries,” said Meghan Swiber, a U.S. rates strategist at Bank of America, noting that the decline is particularly significant given that the Treasury market has roughly tripled in size since the Fed last recorded this level of selling.

The selloff comes as the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have triggered a surge in energy prices and strengthened the dollar, upending the finances of countries that rely on oil imports.

Central banks have intervened in foreign exchange markets to prop up their currencies, a move that typically involves selling U.S. dollars (DXY) and the Treasury securities they hold.

 

 

 

Oil importers such as Turkey, India, and Thailand are likely among those selling as they pay higher dollar-denominated prices for oil, according to Brad Setser, a senior fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations who studies foreign holdings of Treasuries.

Turkey’s central bank has sold $22B of foreign government securities from its foreign currency reserves since Feb. 27, the day before attacks on Iran were launched, with Setser noting a significant portion were likely Treasuries.

“A number of countries…don’t want their currencies to weaken further because it pushes up the local currency price of oil—and either means more fiscal subsidies or more pain for households,” he explained.

The selling is adding pressure to an already volatile Treasury market, with yields on both two-year (US2Y) and 10-year Treasuries (US10Y) rising this month by the most since 2024, increasing borrowing costs for the government, businesses, and households.

Stephen Jones, chief investment officer at Aegon Asset Management, suggested the data indicates foreign official holders could be “stocking the war chest” by cashing out their rainy-day funds amid market uncertainty.

The recent selloff reflects a broader trend of foreign reserve managers diversifying away from dollars and Treasuries, which has made foreign private investors an increasingly important part of the $30 trillion market.

“The recent selling speaks to the larger story, which is that foreign reserve managers and official accounts are diversifying away from Treasuries,” Swiber said, underscoring the shifting dynamics of global demand for the preeminent reserve asset.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR), on the other hand, has topped Treasuries in global central bank reserves, with world central banks holding 24% of total reserves in the precious metal vs. 21% in Treasuries, according to the World Gold Council.

 

 

 

Treasury ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), and (BIL).

U.S. Treasury yields: (US2Y), (US5Y), (US10Y), (US20Y), (US30Y)

data.title: Weekly ETF flows: six of 11 sectors record outflows; the financial sector leads inflows
data.description:
The world's largest exchange-traded fund, SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY), saw inflows of $8.98B for the week ending March 27, while its price decreased by 3.25%.

iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) recorded inflows totaling $370.91M, while its price increased by 1.55% during the week. On the other hand, the Gold SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) recorded outflows totaling $1.08B last week while GLD prices rose 2.64% during the week. 

iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) saw an outflow of $2.49M last week, while Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slipped 5.93% during the same period.

Last week’s inflows/outflows

The 11 S&P 500 sector tracking ETFs collectively recorded inflows of about $200.21M last week, according to data from etfdb.com.

The Financial Sector (XLF) led sector inflows totaling $975M, while six out of 11 sectors saw money flowing out of their respective sector-wise funds. The Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI) reported a weekly inflow of $435.49M, followed by the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLC) with inflows of $95.04M.

The biggest outflows last week were seen in the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP), totaling $605.91M, followed by the Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLB) with outflows of $250.24M. The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU) recorded an outflow of $239.42M last week.

data.title: Amazon to launch new small business credit cards in partnership with U.S. Bank, Mastercard
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN) on Tuesday said it is transitioning its small business credit cards to U.S. Bank, a unit of U.S. Bancorp (USB), and Mastercard (MA).

Amazon announced a new Prime Business Card and a new Amazon Business Card, which will be issued by U.S. Bank as part of the Mastercard network. The cards will come this spring.

The Prime Business Card offers Prime members 5% back on Amazon purchases, while the Amazon Business Card offers 3% back for customers without a Prime membership.

"Small businesses told us they wanted more ways to earn rewards wherever they shop and better tools to manage cash flow. We partnered with U.S. Bank and Mastercard to build a card program that delivers exactly that," said Tai Koottatep, director and general manager of Worldwide B2B Payments & Lending at Amazon.

data.title: Wendy's licensed business in Japan could be in play
data.description:
The Japanese unit of Wendy's Company (WEN) is in play, according to Bloomberg. Investment firm Longreach Group is reportedly considering offers. 

"Private equity is always looking for any overlooked, undervalued asset class, and certainly the restaurant industry has been that," stated Wendy's Japan Chairman Ernie Higa on the development.

Wendy's (WEN) entered Japan in 1980 through a franchise deal with Daiei Group, but the brand never gained the scale or market fit it needed in the highly competitive fast-food environment. The chain struggled for years against more established U.S. rivals such as McDonald’s, and by 2009 Wendy’s ended its first Japan run after the franchise agreement lapsed.

Wendy's (WEN) returned to Japan in 2011 under a new arrangement tied to Ernest Higa and Higa Industries, but the comeback was initially small and slow, with only a few stores open in the early years. To strengthen the business, Wendy’s Japan later acquired First Kitchen in 2016 and created the hybrid "Wendy’s First Kitchen" concept, aiming to blend Wendy’s (WEN) brand with a more localized menu and restaurant style. Since then, the Japan business has focused on a premium, fast-casual positioning rather than a pure U.S.-style burger rollout. 

Shares of Wendy's (WEN) were up 0.9% in Tuesday afternoon trading.

data.title: Google unveils its most cost-effective AI video model Veo 3.1 Lite
data.description:
On Tuesday, Google unveiled Veo 3.1 Lite, its most cost-effective AI video model, and announced plans to cut prices of Veo 3.1 Fast.

Shares of Google jumped about 5% on Tuesday.

The Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) unit said that this model empowers developers to build high-volume video applications at less than 50% of the cost of Veo 3.1 Fast, but with the same speed. This rounds out the Veo 3.1 model family, giving developers flexibility based on needs, according to the company.

The U.S. tech giant also said that on April 7, it will also reduce the pricing for Veo 3.1 Fast, allowing for even more developers to integrate video generation into their products.

Veo 3.1 Lite supports text-to-video and image-to-video. It offers flexible framing for landscape (16:9) and portrait (9:16) ratios and 720p and 1080p video resolutions. Developers can also customize the duration to four, six, or eight seconds, with the cost adjusting accordingly.

The model is rolling out today, and people can access the model through paid tiers on the Gemini API and Google AI Studio.

Last week, OpenAI (OPENAI) said it plans to discontinue offering its AI-powered short video generation app Sora. "As we focus and compute demand grows, the Sora research team continues to focus on world simulation research to advance robotics that will help people solve real-world, physical tasks," an OpenAI spokesperson had told Seeking Alpha in an email.

data.title: Elauwit outlines 15% construction gross margin target while expanding Network-as-a-Service with 3-6 month revenue start
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Elauwit Connection, Inc. (ELWT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Daniel McDonough framed Elauwit as a broadband infrastructure provider to multifamily and student housing and said the company generates recurring revenue under managed services and Network-as-a-Service (NaaS), adding, “we have a robust and quickly expanding pipeline of new installations, giving us visibility into our growth ahead.”
  • McDonough detailed the resident/onboarding value proposition and said the internet fee is typically “included on their rent invoice as a standard cost, but usually at a 10% to 15% less expense than the conventional products,” while also stating Elauwit shares recurring revenue with property owners.
  • Chief Growth Officer Sebastian Shahvandi said the RevOps go-to-market buildout is now producing measurable pipeline, including “approximately 1,800 units in our active pipeline” sourced from early event efforts, “about 6,000 units in active bidding sourced via paid ads,” and “almost 7,000 units of new business pipeline attributed to the partner activity.”
  • CEO Barry Rubens said the company is “excited…to deploy our expanded balance sheet for growth,” adding that being listed “provides the access to capital to expand our market reach and drive growth,” and said the enhanced balance sheet supports pursuing “the 70% of the market opportunity” via NaaS.
  • Rubens disclosed operating unit metrics as of December 31, 2025: contracted units (including units served) “increased 34% to 34,067,” activated units “increased 92% to 22,255,” and billed units “increased 77% to 16,445.” He also said that from “the 121,000 units in our pipeline,” the company has “9,221 units in the contracting process” and “32,968 in the proposal phase.”
  • CFO Sean Arnette said Q4 revenue “increased 85% to $6.1 million,” and added, “network construction activity, both in terms of cost and margin can be lumpy and incur substantial costs upfront, but leads to long-lived recurring revenue.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal 2026 revenue or EPS guidance in this transcript.
  • Rubens said recurring revenue mix is expected to rise over “the coming years” due to “the rising number of billed units on long-term multiyear contracts” and “the rising contribution of…Network-as-a-Service installations that bill typically at a higher monthly rate,” and added, “We anticipate that recurring revenue will grow steadily… and may be enhanced further by the shift in favor of Network-as-a-Service throughout 2026 and well into 2027.”
  • Shahvandi provided a timing framework for NaaS revenue start: “post contract signing, you can think about 3 to 6 months to starting the revenue side.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter, the company again emphasized that the IPO-related balance sheet enables access to the “other 70%” of the market via NaaS; unlike Q3, management did not discuss an upcoming plan to initiate annual guidance on a future call.

Financial Results

  • Revenue was $6.1 million vs. $5.406 million (analystsEstimates, fiscal Q4 2025).
  • Arnette reported Q4 cost of revenue of $5.5 million, gross profit of $0.5 million, and gross margin of 8.6%, and said, “Management is currently implementing cost reduction actions intended to bring our network construction gross margin back into our expected range of approximately 15%.”
  • Arnette reported Q4 operating expenses of $2.8 million, operating loss of $2.2 million, net loss of $2.3 million, and adjusted EBITDA loss of $2.2 million, and attributed results to “our investment in our sales and marketing teams as well as public company-related expenses.”
  • For the full year 2025, Arnette reported revenue of $21.6 million, cost of revenue of $17.6 million, gross profit of $4.0 million, gross margin of 18.5%, and operating expenses of $7.7 million, citing increased activations and higher-margin recurring services as contributors to the full-year margin profile.

Q&A

  • Logan (for George Sutton), Craig-Hallum: Asked how quickly “9,000 units in the contracting phase” and “32,000 units in the proposal phase” could move into contracted units, and about timing to win incremental bidding opportunities; CEO Barry Rubens replied, “The majority of the 9,200 units in the contracting process will be complete…by the end of April,” and added that applying a “25%” success rate to the proposal pool “would represent another 8,000 units that we'd expect to have contracted before the end of the year.”
  • Logan (for George Sutton), Craig-Hallum: Asked about large property managers moving portfolios to managed WiFi; CEO Rubens said, “The process…is accelerating,” adding the shift “could have a material impact on our results looking at 2026 and 2027,” and cited a customer comment about capturing “the 200 basis points of NOI that's available to them.”
  • Logan (for George Sutton), Craig-Hallum: Asked how Elauwit plans to increase NaaS from roughly 5% of pipeline; Chief Growth Officer Sebastian Shahvandi said the company is targeting smaller portfolios “where…capital is not readily available,” positioning NaaS as “0 capital expenditures from their side.”
  • Derek Greenberg, Maxim Group: Asked when NaaS begins generating revenue; Shahvandi answered, “you can look at 3 to 6 months for it to get started,” and said NaaS conversations “move a little bit faster than new builds.”
  • Derek Greenberg, Maxim Group: Asked how much Q4 G&A was one-time IPO-related and the run-rate; CFO Sean Arnette said “in between 15% and 20%” of the increase was one-time and that the company expects SG&A “to come down a bit as we move into 2026.”
  • Derek Greenberg, Maxim Group: Asked sales and marketing spend at scale and margin potential as recurring revenue scales; Shahvandi said, “for 2026, I think it's around $1.5 million for sales and marketing combined,” Arnette added SG&A is expected to be “about 20% of the expense of the business,” and Arnette said the long-term margin framework is unchanged: “around 15% gross margin on our network construction activities,” with managed services “in the neighborhood of 60%” and NaaS “closer to 75% over time.”
  • Deane Pernis, Pernis Research: Asked prospect awareness of offerings and how growth will be financed; Shahvandi said positioning depends on “the portfolio size” and that Elauwit presents both models, while CFO Arnette said NaaS projects are expected to be funded “predominantly from debt with a small bit of equity capital off the balance sheet,” and noted an existing relationship “with Endurance Financial.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and detail-seeking, concentrating on pipeline conversion speed, NaaS revenue timing, cost normalization post-IPO, and profitability mechanics; questions repeatedly pressed for timelines and cost structure (e.g., Maxim asking what expenses “revert to or hover around”).
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was positive and expansion-oriented, emphasizing scale and access to capital, with Rubens saying the company is “excited…to deploy our expanded balance sheet for growth.” In Q&A, responses were more operational and specific on timing/cost, including Arnette’s “15% to 20%…one-time” IPO cost characterization.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management provided more quantified pipeline and conversion timing detail (units “by the end of April,” and NaaS “3 to 6 months”), compared with Q3 where McDonough said the company was “really reluctant to quantify things at this moment.”

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current call added a dedicated sales/marketing operating update from Chief Growth Officer Sebastian Shahvandi, aligning with the prior quarter’s expectation that the new CGO would “join these calls to provide additional color.”
  • Management shifted from Q3’s limited willingness to quantify pipeline to Q4’s concrete funnel reporting (e.g., “9,221 units in the contracting process” and “32,968 in the proposal phase”) and near-term conversion expectations (“by the end of April”).
  • Profitability discussion shifted from Q3’s emphasis on gross margin expansion and “positive adjusted EBITDA” to Q4’s lower construction-heavy gross margin (8.6%) and explicit cost actions to return construction margin to “approximately 15%,” alongside higher operating expense tied to sales/marketing buildout.
  • Analysts maintained similar themes quarter to quarter—unit conversion timing, NaaS rollout, and economics—while Q4 questioning increased focus on expense normalization and long-term margin structure.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Arnette warned that “network construction activity…can be lumpy and incur substantial costs upfront,” with near-term pressure visible in Q4 margins; he said mitigation is underway via “cost reduction actions” targeting construction gross margin back to “approximately 15%.”
  • Management highlighted reliance on successful scaling of recurring revenue and pipeline conversion, with Rubens noting activated units represent a “rollover period” and that onboarding aligns billing with lease renewals over time.
  • Financing execution for NaaS buildouts remains a focus area discussed in Q&A, with Arnette indicating the company expects to fund projects “predominantly from debt” while working with “a variety of different type of capital partners.”

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that the IPO-enhanced balance sheet is being used to broaden access to NaaS opportunities and to fund growth, while the newly launched RevOps engine is producing quantified pipeline signals. Executives highlighted rapid expansion in contracted, activated, and billed units as of year-end 2025, but paired that growth narrative with near-term profitability pressures from construction mix, describing margin as lumpy and outlining actions to return construction gross margin to roughly 15%, with longer-term targets tied to higher-margin recurring services and NaaS adoption moving through 2026 and 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BIO-key anticipates Q1 2026 revenue of approximately $2.2M, targeting breakeven and profitability in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BIO-key International (BKYI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • “we now anticipate Q1 '26 revenue of approximately $2.2 million, representing a 37% increase over Q1 and 2025 and a larger sequential improvement over Q4 '25 as well,” said Chairman & CEO Michael DePasquale, adding, “we also expect a substantial improvement in our Q1 '26 bottom line performance exceeding each of our fiscal '25 quarters.”
  • DePasquale framed 2025’s revenue pressure around two items “totaling roughly $2 million,” including “a $1.5 million 2-year license renewal with a foreign national bank” largely recorded in 2024; he said the same customer “executed an expanded 1-year license renewal of over $1 million for 2026.”
  • On strategy, DePasquale said 2025 reflected “the completion in 2025 of our strategic transition to selling only BIO-key-branded solutions in the EMEA region,” and argued the shift is “beginning to benefit our gross margin and growth prospects” as the company rebuilds pipeline around higher net margin offerings.
  • Product updates emphasized regulated verticals. DePasquale said BIO-key “formally introduced the new FBI FAP 20 Certified EcoID III fingerprint scanner,” describing encrypted device-to-host communication and liveness detection, and said PortalGuard “Version 7.0… is currently undergoing comprehensive third-party security testing for an expected release during the second quarter.”
  • On cost and liquidity, DePasquale said, “in 2025, we were able to reduce our total SG&A expense by almost $800,000 or 11%,” and closed the year with “$2.7 million in cash,” while “increasing our book value to $7.6 million.”
  • “the total 2025 revenues decreased 12% to $6.1 million versus $6.9 million in 2024,” said Chief Financial Officer Cecilia Welch, adding that Hardware revenue “increased over 100%… to $1.3 million” and Service revenue “increased 6% to $1.2 million.”

Outlook

  • Management gave a near-term revenue target rather than full-year guidance: DePasquale said, “we now anticipate Q1 '26 revenue of approximately $2.2 million,” and said Q1 mix should skew toward licensing: “the majority will likely be License revenue, but there's also some strong Hardware revenue as well.”
  • On the path to profitability, DePasquale reiterated an objective rather than a dated commitment: “our goal this year, right, is to be breakeven or profitable and to be cash flow positive,” and later added, “we should be there in the early part of 2026. That's our goal and objective.”
  • Versus the prior quarter’s posture, DePasquale’s Q3 2025 call discussed revenue “lumpiness” and said PortalGuard Version 7 general availability was expected “late Q1 or early Q2 in 2026”; in Q4 2025, he tightened the expected timing to “an expected release during the second quarter.”
  • Analysts’ estimates were not provided in the prompt, so no comparison to Street expectations was included.

Financial Results

  • Welch reported 2025 gross margin of “77.5% as compared to 81.4% in 2024,” attributing the change “primarily due to the mix” between software license fees and hardware; she also stated, “Gross margins on license fee improved 91% in 2025 from 88% in 2024,” tied to the EMEA branded-product transition.
  • Welch said operating discipline continued: “we reduced our SG&A costs by 11%,” R&D “increased 4%,” and “operating expenses decreased 7% overall in 2025.”
  • Welch reported profitability metrics: “net loss increased to $4.6 million or $0.69 per share from $4.3 million or $2.09 per share in 2024,” and “BIO-key's Q4 '25 net loss increased to $1.7 million or $0.19 per share as compared to the $1.4 million in 2024 or $0.46 per share.”
  • Balance sheet items included “current assets of $4.6 million, including cash of $2.7 million,” and “Accounts receivable increased 73% to $1.2 million at December 31, 2025.”

Q&A

  • Jack Vander Aarde, Maxim Group LLC: Asked for more detail on the foreign bank revenue timing and whether it renews in 2026; Chairman & CEO DePasquale: “they did renew for 1 year at over $1 million… about a 30% increase,” and clarified, “We took the revenue all in 2024 for that 2-years… that's why… in 2025… it wasn't repeatable.”
  • Vander Aarde, Maxim Group LLC: Asked about Q1 2026 revenue composition and whether any Q4 slippage contributed; Chairman & CEO DePasquale: “the majority will likely be License revenue,” with “some strong Hardware revenue as well,” and pointed to blended margins typically “never lower than the high 70s… up through the low 80s.”
  • Vander Aarde, Maxim Group LLC: Asked where the largest “needle mover” opportunities sit across regions and verticals; Chairman & CEO DePasquale: cited “a niche in defense and in government,” “very large opportunities in South America,” and said TD Synnex would act “as a force multiplier.”
  • Dan Camis (private investor): Asked if Q1 expenses were similar to Q4; Chairman & CEO DePasquale: “we haven't reported the quarter. So I can't comment on the exact numbers,” but said the quarter “should be similar to all of the other quarters” with potential website relaunch expense.
  • Camis (private investor): Pressed on R&D trajectory and what’s “revolutionary” in PortalGuard Version 7; Chairman & CEO DePasquale: said most spend “has already been spent” and expected R&D to be “relatively stable,” and called Version 7 “significant,” highlighting “multi-tenant management” so partners can “deploy licenses, pull them back… and… utilize those licenses in other accounts.”
  • Camis (private investor): Asked about ARR and passwordless adoption proof points; Chairman & CEO DePasquale: “we're in that range” of “$6 million to $7 million,” and said on U.S. demand, “Yes. No doubt. New business, no question, yes.”
  • Camis (private investor): Asked about reverse split/Nasdaq compliance; Chairman & CEO DePasquale: “We're not going to risk the potential to lose our NASDAQ listing,” adding, “if we need to, we certainly will,” while noting, “If we have until early May… to have the stock trade for 10 consecutive days over $1… we certainly would not do the reverse split.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts tone was slightly positive and inquisitive, with Vander Aarde calling the Q1 target “fantastic” and focusing on revenue timing, mix, and pipeline visibility.
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, with DePasquale stating, “Significant growth and profitability are in sight,” while also drawing firm lines on undisclosed data: “we haven't reported the quarter. So I can't comment on the exact numbers.”
  • Compared with Q3 2025, management language in Q4 2025 leaned more emphatically toward near-term acceleration (explicit Q1 revenue target) and operational footing (cash, book value), while Q3 emphasized variability and financing runway.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2025 added an explicit near-term revenue target (“approximately $2.2 million”) and tied it to both year-over-year and sequential growth, while Q3 2025 discussed quarterly variability and said it expected to “close out the year very strong” without giving a specific quarterly revenue target.
  • Q4 2025 highlighted the EMEA transition as completed and margin-accretive, whereas Q3 2025 described EMEA as “focused solely on BIO-key branded solutions” and emphasized channel momentum.
  • Q4 2025 introduced heightened Nasdaq compliance focus via the reverse split discussion; Q3 2025 Q&A did not include listing compliance as a topic.

Risks and Concerns

  • DePasquale acknowledged execution timing risk in EMEA, saying the transition “took a little bit longer than we expected,” and described deal sizes as often “in the high hundreds of thousands of dollars,” creating timing variability.
  • In response to expense questions, DePasquale noted reporting constraints: “we haven't reported the quarter,” limiting visibility into near-term cost cadence.
  • On listing risk mitigation, DePasquale said the company would act to avoid delisting: “We're not going to risk the potential to lose our NASDAQ listing.”

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a sharper near-term setup for 2026, anchored by DePasquale’s expectation of “approximately $2.2 million” in Q1 2026 revenue and an improving bottom line, while attributing 2025 softness to revenue timing from a large foreign bank renewal and a longer-than-expected EMEA transition to BIO-key-branded offerings. Alongside product milestones (EcoID III and PortalGuard Version 7 targeting a Q2 release), management highlighted cost reductions, $2.7 million of cash, and a stated intent to protect the Nasdaq listing if needed through a reverse split authorization, while reiterating a goal of reaching breakeven/profitability and cash-flow positivity in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ETFs tied to Marvell Technology jumped following Nvidia investment news
data.description:
Shares of Marvell Technology (MRVL) surged 11.3% Tuesday afternoon following NVIDIA’s (NVDA) announcement of a $2B investment and an expanded strategic partnership. 

The collaboration will integrate Marvell’s capabilities into NVIDIA’s AI infrastructure ecosystem through NVLink Fusion, reinforcing Marvell’s positioning within next-generation data center architecture. 

The move also lifted a broad range of exchange-traded funds with meaningful exposure to Marvell Technology. ETFs such as the Direxion Daily MRVL Bull 2X ETF (MRVU) and several semiconductor-focused funds posted notable gains as the stock rallied. 

Marvell remains widely held across the ETF landscape, with 292 funds collectively owning roughly 145.6M shares, underscoring its growing importance within AI-driven investment themes. 

Outlined below are the 10 ETFs with the largest portfolio allocations towards shares of MRVL: 

  1. Direxion Daily MRVL Bull 2X ETF (MRVU), 10.73% allocation.
  2. Fidelity Disruptive Technology ETF (FDTX), 5.63% allocation.
  3. iShares MSCI Global Sustainable Development Goals ETF (SDG), 5.11% allocation. 
  4. U.S. Global Technology and Aerospace & Defense ETF (WAR), 5.07% allocation.
  5. iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), 5.04% allocation.
  6. Invesco PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXQ), 5.03% allocation.
  7. VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF (SMHX), 4.99% allocation. 
  8. First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF (FTXL), 4.94% allocation.
  9. TCW Transform Systems ETF (PWRD), 4.91% allocation. 
  10. iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY), 4.88% allocation.
